original_abstract: Abstract After a decade of crisis management, the democratic implications of emergency modes of governance in the European Union (EU) are under the spotlight. The prevailing analysis is critical. Scholars point to an emergent, distinctly European trend of transnational crisis exploitation where elite appeals to exceptional pressures serve asymmetric power and influence, overriding democratic norms and potentially fuelling Eurosceptic backlash. However, the literature does not ask whether citizens consider themselves disempowered by the EU’s emergency politics, with its alleged emphasis on urgency and technocratic problem-solving. The relative symmetry and simultaneity of the Covid-19 crisis across Europe offers an opportunity for an empirical examination of public opinion on traits of emergency politics. We juxtapose the implications of emergency politics for public opinion with the transnational cleavages literature and use survey data from 15 member states on EU- and national-level pandemic responses to examine the competing hypotheses. Our findings indicate perceptions of crisis management are largely determined by prior views on EU integration and democracy. More generally, the results suggest that the transnational cleavage remains overall a key driver and delimiter of Euroscepticism in crisis times. Though there is some variance between emergency politics dimensions, we do not detect a widespread perception of disillusionment motivated by EU emergency rule.
humanized_abstract: Dis-Enfranchising Europeans through Emergency Politics? The COVID-19 Pandemic in EU Member States Abstract Over a decade of crisis management in the European Union (EU) has prompted a focus on whether and how democratic processes are eroded in emergency mode governance. The current academic consensus is one of warning in relation to a new form of transnational ‘crisis governance exploitation’ that uses crisis rhetoric for elite purposes in an asymmetrical manner to the detriment of EU citizens and at the same time in so doing exacerbates Euroscepticism. However, there is no research to our knowledge into the extent to which EU citizens perceive themselves to be disenfranchised through emergency politics governance which arguably promotes speed, pragmatism and technocratic governance. With the crisis being experienced uniformly and in tandem across Europe the COVID-19 pandemic presents an unprecedented opportunity to look into the extent to which citizens have concerns with elements of emergency politics as currently instantiated in the EU context. Our research examines public opinion in relation to elements of emergency politics against the backdrop of the transnational cleavages theory. It is grounded in public opinion surveys concerning EU citizens’ perceptions of the national and EU-wide management of the pandemic, in the 15 Member States with available data. Our analysis shows that citizens’ views concerning EU management of crisis are primarily a reproduction of their existing attitudes towards EU membership and EU citizenship. We find only a minor part of citizens’ attitudes towards emergency politics to be outside of the scope of these traditional EU-citizenship transnational cleavages. Our research does not confirm citizens feeling disenfranchised or losing faith in EU institutions through emergency politics governance, at least not at this stage in the trajectory of the crisis governance response in the EU. The EU-citizenship related transnational cleavage of the EU, also appears to remain a structuring and limiting factor to the development of alternative and possible additional transnational identity and citizenship-based cleavages in times of crisis, too. Furthermore, it also does not confirm that citizens’ attitudes towards EU emergency politics are meaningfully and differently influenced by any of the individual emergency politics governance features we examine.

document.input: Abstract After a decade of crisis management, the democratic implications of emergency modes of governance in the European Union (EU) are under the spotlight.
